title: Testing whether compost is ready to use
subtitle: Visual, smell, and simple bag tests for mature compost
excerpt: Immature compost continues to break down after application, competing with plants for nitrogen and potentially harming seeds. Three tests, appearance, smell, and the bag test, confirm whether a compost heap has reached a stable, usable state. None requires equipment beyond a plastic bag.
sourceNotes: WRAP compost maturity criteria. RHS compost-readiness guidance (rhs.org.uk). Brinton, W.F., Compost maturity index (2000).

content.text: Compost that has not fully matured still holds active break-down products like organic acids and ammonia, plus bits of material that have not finished breaking down. Put on the soil too early, it can stop seeds from germinating and tie up nitrogen as the bacteria keep working. The tests below tell ready compost from compost that needs more time.

Mature compost is dark brown to black, crumbly, and homogeneous. No individual pieces of raw material should be recognisable. If you can still see intact vegetable peelings, cardboard structure, or identifiable plant material, the compost is not ready. The one exception is woody material (sticks, large cardboard pieces) which often outlasts the rest, remove and return it to the next heap rather than waiting for it.

Mature compost smells of forest floor, earthy, slightly sweet, neutral. If it smells of ammonia, sulphur, or decay, it is still active and not ready. If it smells of nothing, it may be ready or may be too dry (add water and retest in a week). The earthy smell is produced by actinomycetes bacteria, which colonise compost in its final maturation phase.

Place a handful of compost in a sealed plastic bag. Leave for one week at room temperature. At the end of the week: if white mould has grown, the compost is still active (fungi are continuing to break it down) and needs more time. If no mould has grown and the compost looks the same as when you sealed it, it is stable and ready to use.

heap, temperature is the clearest sign. A heap that has settled below 40°C after the final turn, and does not heat up again when turned, has finished the hot phase. It is not yet fully mature. The heap still needs 2 to 4 weeks of curing (left undisturbed) before the bag test confirms it is stable.
What to do with not-quite-ready compost

Compost that fails the bag test can be used as a surface mulch under established trees and shrubs, where it will continue maturing in place without contacting seed or fine roots. It should not be dug into vegetable beds or used as a seed-sowing medium.
